title: Roo
text:
soft mind, sharp thinking

    
      ☁ Fintech
      ⊕ Risk & AML
      ·· Data Stories
      ◈ Tech + Aesthetics
    

    scroll to explore ↓
  








  
    Hello
    I'm Roopa — Roo.
    
    
      Masters student at SMU, with an interest in
      understanding financial crime and studying the intersection
      of technology, finance, and design.
      I like to build things that are impactful and beautiful —
      data tells many stories; it is a matter of perspective,
      and that is what I like to explore.
    
  





  
    Independent Work · Fintech & Financial Crime
    Projects
    
      Self-directed explorations across AML typologies, risk analytics,
      and emerging areas in financial crime — built to go beyond the classroom
      and demonstrate genuine curiosity about where the field is heading.
    
  

  
  
    01
    
      
        Art Markets · PEP Networks
        R · Tableau · ICIJ · AML
      
      Art Markets & the PEP Problem
      
        The art market's opacity makes it a documented vehicle for laundering —
        especially among politically exposed persons. Combining auction data,
        ICIJ offshore leaks, and PEP network analysis to visualise how money
        and influence move through high-value cultural assets.
      
    
    →
  

  
  
    02
    
      
        Trade Finance · Mirror Analysis
        Python · UN Comtrade · FATF
      
      Trade-Based Money Laundering
      
        TBML is one of the most significant and least visible ML typologies —
        manipulating trade invoices to move value across borders. Using UN Comtrade
        mirror trade data, this project surfaces discrepancies across high-risk
        corridors and maps them to FATF and MAS Notice 626 frameworks.
      
    
    →
  

  
  
    03
    
      
        On-Chain Analytics · Regulation
        Python · Etherscan · Research
      
      Crypto & Financial Crime
      
        An honest exploration of on-chain analytics and the evolving regulatory
        landscape for virtual assets. Part research, part hands-on — tracing
        transaction flows, understanding mixer patterns, and mapping where
        MAS and FATF guidance currently stands.
      
    
    →

title: Roopa Kaustubha Rayudu
text:
Roo's Observatory · Est. 2026
    Roopa Kaustubha Rayudu
    soft mind, sharp thinking
    
      I make things that are impactful and beautiful — or at least try to.
      Currently doing my Masters in IT in Business (Fintech) at SMU, building toward
      a career in risk and financial crime analytics. I'm drawn to the idea that
      rigorous thinking and careful aesthetics aren't opposites —
      the best work lives in that intersection.
    
  

  
  
    Trajectory

    
      Tata Consultancy Services · Citi Bank — Singapore
      
        Assistant Systems Engineer
        Aug 2024 – Dec 2025
      
      Banking Environment Support & Risk Coordination
      
        Supported multiple banking applications across SIT and UAT environments, ensuring operational readiness before production releases.
        Managed certificate lifecycle governance, reducing authentication failures and operational risk across banking platforms.
        Maintained 99%+ environment stability during release cycles through proactive cross-functional coordination.
        Resolved high-priority incidents within SLA timelines, minimising impact to business testing and regulatory reporting schedules.
        Maintained audit-ready incident and change documentation in ServiceNow, aligned with internal control frameworks.
      
    

    
      Fergus Consultancy Group — Singapore
      
        Management Consultant
        Dec 2023 – Apr 2024
      
      
        Advised clients across technology, healthcare, and financial services on regulatory documentation and compliance processes.
        Interpreted regulatory frameworks and aligned client documentation with statutory and governance requirements.
        Reduced compliance-related processing delays by 30% through improved documentation clarity and workflow redesign.
      
    
  

  

  
  
    Formation

    
      Master of IT in Business — Financial Technology & Analytics
      Singapore Management University (SMU) · Jan 2026 – Jun 2027
      Fintech track · Second semester
    

    
      Bachelor of Business Administration
      ICFAI Business School, Hyderabad · Sep 2020 – May 2023
      GPA 3.50 / 4.00 · 5 merit scholarships across first four semesters
    
  

  

  
  
    Instruments
    
      
        Programming
        Python · SQL · R · Java
      
      
        Platforms & Tools
        Splunk · ServiceNow · Jira · Git · Jenkins · Ansible · MySQL
      
      
        Analytics & Viz
        Tableau · Excel Modelling · Quarto · R
      
      
        Domain
        AML Typologies · KYC / CDD · SIT / UAT · Regulatory Compliance
      
      
        Languages
        English · Hindi · Telugu
      
    
  

  

  
  
    Credentials
    
      ICA Certificate in KYC & Customer Due Diligence
      M5 — Financial Advisory Regulations (MAS)
      M9 / M9A — Life Insurance & Investment-Linked Products (MAS)
    
  

  
  
    Beyond the telescope
    
      I am, at heart, a reader and a thinker. History, philosophy, literary fiction —
      I'm drawn to anything that tries to make sense of why things are the way they are.
      I think good storytelling and good analysis aren't so different: both are about
      finding the pattern underneath the surface, and then finding the right form
      to show it to someone else.

title: Crypto & Financial Crime
text:
← All Projects
      
      Exploration · Research & Hands-On · Crypto AML
      Crypto & Financial Crime
      
        An honest, in-progress exploration of on-chain analytics and the evolving
        regulatory landscape for virtual assets. Part research, part hands-on —
        tracing transaction flows on public blockchains, understanding how mixing
        and layering work in practice, and mapping where MAS and FATF guidance
        currently stands and where it doesn't.
      
      
      
        Python
        Etherscan API
        On-Chain Analytics
        FATF Virtual Assets
        Ongoing ◉
  

  
  

    
    
      
        I
        
      
      
        Why this, and why this framing
        
          Crypto compliance is the fastest-growing area in financial crime, and MAS is
          actively building its regulatory framework for digital payment token service
          providers under the Payment Services Act. But it is also genuinely complex —
          the technology moves faster than regulation, the terminology is contested, and
          a lot of what's written about it is either too technical or too superficial.
        
        
          This is an exploration, not a finished project. The framing matters: I'd rather
          show the process of learning something rigorously than pretend to expertise I
          don't yet have.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        II
        
      
      
        Research thread
        
          The written component maps the current regulatory landscape: FATF's updated
          virtual asset guidance (2021, revised 2023), the travel rule and its
          implementation challenges across jurisdictions, MAS's Notice PSN02 and how
          Singapore's framework compares to the EU's MiCA. It also covers the core AML
          typologies specific to virtual assets — mixer and tumbler usage, chain-hopping,
          peer-to-peer networks, and DeFi as a layering vehicle.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        III
        
      
      
        Hands-on thread
        
          Using the Etherscan API and Elliptic's publicly released academic dataset
          (which contains labelled illicit and licit wallet addresses), the practical
          component traces transaction flows around known case studies — the Bitfinex
          hack of 2016 is fully documented on-chain and makes an instructive case study
          for how large-scale layering actually moves through a public ledger. The goal
          is a simple flow visualisation and hop analysis, not a predictive model.
          Seeing the data is the point.
        

        
        
          Data Sources
          
            01
            
              Etherscan API
              Public Ethereum transaction data — wallet balances, transfer histories, contract interactions.
            
          
          
            02
            
              Elliptic Academic Dataset
              Labelled Bitcoin transaction graph with known illicit and licit classifications — used in academic research on on-chain anomaly detection.
            
          
          
            03
            
              MAS / FATF Guidance
              Primary source regulatory documents — FATF virtual asset guidance, MAS PSN02, Payment Services Act — forming the research backbone.
            
          
        

      
    

    
    
      
        IV
        
      
      
        Work so far
        
          Published pieces
          
            01
            
              Virtual Assets & the AML Regime
              Research thread — written essay mapping the regulatory landscape. Covers FATF R.15, the Travel Rule, MAS PSN02, and a comparison with EU MiCA. Draft in progress.
            
          
          
            02
            
              On-Chain Analysis — Bitfinex Case Study
              Hands-on thread — Python notebook with Etherscan and Elliptic dataset analysis. Transaction flow visualisation and hop analysis of the 2016 Bitfinex hack. Coming soon.
            
          
        
      
    

    
    
      ✦
      
        This is a living exploration — research notes, code, and findings
        will be added here as the work develops.

title: Art Markets & the PEP Problem
text:
← All Projects
      
      Visual Analytics · Network Analysis · AML Typologies
      Art Markets & the PEP Problem
      
        The art market's opacity makes it a documented vehicle for laundering —
        especially among politically exposed persons. This project combines auction
        data, ICIJ offshore leaks, and PEP network analysis to visualise how money
        and influence move through high-value cultural assets.
      
      
      
        R
        Tableau
        ICIJ Offshore Leaks
        Network Analysis
        FATF Typologies
        In Progress ◎
      
    
  

  
  

    
    
      
        I
        
      
      
        Why the art market
        
          Art is unusual as an asset class: it is portable, subjective in value,
          and largely unregulated compared to financial instruments of equivalent worth.
          The FATF's 2023 guidance on art market money laundering is explicit — auction
          houses, galleries, and freeport storage facilities represent a documented
          typology vector, particularly for PEPs seeking to move and obscure wealth
          across jurisdictions without triggering standard financial monitoring.
        
        
          The connection between art markets and PEPs is not theoretical. The Panama
          and Pandora Papers contain documented examples of offshore structures used
          to purchase high-value art, with ownership deliberately obscured through
          shell companies and nominee arrangements. This project is an attempt to
          surface that pattern analytically.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        II
        
      
      
        Method & approach
        
          The project works across three linked analytical threads: price anomaly
          detection in public auction records, geographic mapping of freeport flows
          (Geneva, Singapore, Luxembourg, Delaware), and cross-referencing against
          ICIJ offshore entity data and the OpenSanctions PEP database to identify
          counterparty overlap.
        
        
          Where a named auction buyer or seller appears in both public art market
          records and ICIJ offshore data — or maps to a known PEP entity — that
          intersection becomes the unit of analysis. The output is not a legal
          accusation; it's a demonstration of what the data makes visible when
          you connect sources that are rarely put side by side.
        

        
        
          Data Sources
          
            01
            
              ICIJ Offshore Leaks Database
              Panama Papers, Pandora Papers — offshore entity structures, beneficial ownership networks, and jurisdiction mapping.
            
          
          
            02
            
              Public Auction Records
              Christie's and Sotheby's publicly available sale results — lot prices, sale dates, buyer/seller regions for anomaly analysis.
            
          
          
            03
            
              OpenSanctions PEP Database
              Structured dataset of politically exposed persons across jurisdictions — used for counterparty cross-referencing and network construction.
            
          
          
            04
            
              Basel Art Market Report
              Annual market sizing and geographic flow data — contextual baseline for understanding scale and opacity of the global art trade.
            
          
        

      
    

    
    
      
        III
        
      
      
        Expected output
        
          An interactive Tableau dashboard visualising auction price anomalies and
          geographic freeport flows alongside an R network visualisation of PEP
          counterparty connections. The written analysis maps findings to FATF's
          2023 art market ML typology guidance — grounding the visual work in
          regulatory vocabulary relevant to CDD and EDD practice.
        
        
          The goal is not to reinvent forensic accounting. It is to show what becomes
          visible when you combine public data sources with AML typology thinking —
          and to practice the kind of cross-source analysis that underlies real
          financial crime intelligence work.
        
      
    

    
    
      ✦
      
        Work in progress — visualisations and full analysis will be published
        here as the project develops.

title: Trade-Based Money Laundering
text:
← All Projects
      
      Quantitative Analysis · Trade Finance · AML Typologies
      Trade-Based Money Laundering
      
        TBML is one of the most significant and least visible ML typologies —
        manipulating trade invoices to move value across borders. Using UN Comtrade
        mirror trade data, this project surfaces discrepancies across high-risk
        corridors and maps them to FATF and MAS Notice 626 frameworks.
      
      
      
        Python
        UN Comtrade API
        Pandas
        FATF Typologies
        MAS Notice 626
        In Progress ◎
      
    
  

  
  

    
    
      
        I
        
      
      
        Why TBML, and why mirror trade analysis
        
          Trade-based money laundering accounts for a significant share of illicit
          financial flows globally, yet it remains one of the hardest typologies
          to detect — because it hides in plain sight, inside the normal machinery
          of international commerce. Over- and under-invoicing of goods, phantom
          shipments, and multiple invoicing all exploit the opacity of trade
          documentation and the volume of transactions that pass through ports
          every day.
        
        
          Singapore's position as a major trade hub makes this directly relevant.
          MAS Notice 626 and FATF's TBML guidance both identify trade finance
          as a high-risk channel, and the ability to read and flag TBML indicators
          is a concrete, valued skill in compliance roles across the region.
        
        
          Mirror trade analysis is the methodological core: when Country A reports
          exporting $X worth of electronics to Country B, but Country B reports
          importing only $0.6X of electronics from Country A in the same period,
          that discrepancy is a signal. At scale, across bilateral corridors and
          commodity categories, patterns emerge.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        II
        
      
      
        Scope & method
        
          The analysis focuses on Southeast Asian and South Asian bilateral trade
          corridors — geographies with documented TBML exposure and meaningful
          volume in the UN Comtrade dataset. Commodity categories are filtered to
          those historically associated with TBML: electronics, textiles, precious
          metals, and agri-commodities with high price variability.
        
        
          For each corridor and commodity pair, the project calculates discrepancy
          ratios between the export-side and import-side reports, flags outliers
          by magnitude and persistence over time, and cross-references against
          FATF's published TBML typology indicators to assess plausibility of
          ML risk versus statistical noise.
        

        
        
          Data Sources
          
            01
            
              UN Comtrade API
              Bilateral trade statistics reported by both exporting and importing countries — the foundational dataset for mirror trade discrepancy analysis. Free API access.
            
          
          
            02
            
              FATF TBML Guidance
              Published typology indicators for trade-based laundering — used to frame which discrepancy patterns are analytically meaningful versus artefacts of reporting.
            
          
          
            03
            
              MAS Notice 626
              Singapore's AML/CFT notice for banks, with specific provisions on trade finance risk — the local regulatory grounding for the analysis.
            
          
        

      
    

    
    
      
        III
        
      
      
        Expected output
        
          A Python notebook containing the full data pipeline — API pull, cleaning,
          discrepancy calculation, and corridor-level flagging — alongside
          visualisations of discrepancy magnitude across corridors and commodity
          categories. Written commentary maps the findings to MAS Notice 626 red
          flag indicators and FATF TBML typology language, so the output reads as
          both analytical and compliance-literate.
        
        
          The differentiator here is specificity: almost nobody does mirror trade
          analysis as a portfolio project, and doing it well — with genuine data,
          a sound methodology, and regulatory grounding — directly signals
          Singapore-relevant knowledge to any compliance or risk team in the region.
        
      
    

    
    
      ✦
      
        Work in progress — the Python notebook and visualisations will be
        published here as the analysis develops.
